subject: [RFC PATCH v2 2/4] mm/zsmalloc: introduce zs_free_deferred() for async handle freeing

reviews.author: Barry Song
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Barry Song questioned whether having a per-pool workqueue would reduce concurrency in freeing zsmalloc memory, potentially causing issues in cases like zswap or single-zram devices, and suggested an alternative approach to handle batch processing.

Reviewer suggested using a per-size-class workqueue to balance scalability and reduce lock contention across multiple classes, where the locks reside
sentiment_signals: balance between scalability and reducing lock contention
reviews.raw_body:
I believe so, as reported in the original email from Lei and Zhiguo,
which proposed introducing a swap entries list for async free.

---

I guess a per–size-class workqueue might strike a balance
between scalability and reducing lock contention across
multiple classes, where the locks actually reside.

Thanks
Barry

2026-04-22.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new function zs_free_deferred() for asynchronous handle freeing in zsmalloc. The original implementation of locking, grabbing a handle, and unlocking one at a time is inefficient and can be improved by using a workqueue or per-cpu-fying the batches/queues to reduce concurrency issues. An alternative approach suggested is to use two arrays of handles, swapping them under a lock when the defer worker is woken up, and then freeing the handles in the old array.

2026-04-21.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new function zs_free_deferred() for asynchronous handle freeing in zsmalloc. The original implementation of locking, grabbing a handle, and unlocking one at a time is inefficient and can be improved by using a workqueue or per-cpu-fying the batches/queues to reduce concurrency issues. An alternative approach suggested is to use two arrays of handles, swapping them under a lock when the defer worker is woken up, and then freeing the handles in the old array.
